Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: The standard is usually Receive Order Check Credit Approve Order Issue Invoice Receive Payment Close Order All those steps are mandatory no skipping no looping in the middle

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: The nodes are R Receive Order xor credit which is XOR of Check Credit and skip loop approve invoice LOOP of A and I P Receive Payment O Close Order Then the order edges R must come before xor credit xor credit before the loop loop before P P before O

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: XOR means you do exactly one of the two children So here after receiving the order you can either do the Check Credit step OR use the silent transition which is a skip no activity That means you can entirely skip the credit check

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Credit checks are there to make sure the customer can pay If you skip that you might approve orders from customers who cannot pay leading to bad debt revenue loss That is a big compliance and risk issue

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Lets remember what LOOP does in process trees POWL a loop operator A I means you do A then you can repeat I A any number of times

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The loop operator in pm4py process tree where child1 is the do part executed at least once child2 is the redo part So the execution sequence is do child1 then do child2 then do child1 repeated zero or more times

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: For loop approve invoice which is A I that means first you have to Approve Order at least once then you can either end the loop or Issue Invoice then Approve Order again then again Issue Invoice as many times as you want before exiting the loop

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: In standard O2C you approve the order once then issue the invoice once

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: If you can approve the order multiple times why would you reapprove an already approved order That could be a sign of errors Then issuing the invoice multiple times for the same order is a problem the customer might get multiple invoices for the same order leading to duplicate payments disputes or accounting errors

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: But if you do zero repetitions of the redo part then the loop only does A Approve Order no I Issue Invoice

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The minimal trace for the loop is just A So that means in this case the loop approve invoice can produce either just Approve Order or Approve Order Issue Invoice Approve Order and so on

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: That means you can have a process where after receiving the order you either check credit or not then approve the order then receive payment then close no invoice ever issued

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: That is a major anomaly Because you approved the order but never issued an invoice then received payment That is a big accounting issue The customer has no formal record of what they are being charged for leading to disputes delayed payments or refusal to pay

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: This model allows skipping two critical steps credit check invoice issuance and repeating two steps approval invoicing multiple times That is a big departure from the standard

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: The silent transition for skipping credit means in event logs there is no record that the credit check was skipped That makes conformance checking and auditing harder because you cannot easily distinguish a skipped step from missing data

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: These anomalies could be intentional workarounds such as skipping credit checks to speed up order processing or reissuing invoices instead of voiding incorrect ones Or they could be modeling errors that introduce unintended process paths

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: The anomalies are Optional Credit Check via XOR with Silent Skip and Uncontrolled LOOP Between Approve Order and Issue Invoice The loop allows both skipping invoicing entirely and repeated approval and invoicing

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: These anomalies make the process model overly permissive allowing noncompliant and inefficient behavior In real world operations they increase financial risk compliance exposure operational costs and customer dissatisfaction
